channel.title: SLM on Brian Hengen's AI Experiments
channel.description: Recent content in SLM on Brian Hengen's AI Experiments

item.title: I Let Two AIs Talk to Each Other for 500 Turns. It Got Weird.
item.description: What happens when you let two local LLMs have unsupervised conversations? Cosmic harmony engines, fictional PhD consultants, and a surprisingly deep discussion about why chickens cross roads.
item.content:encoded:
⚡ TL;DR
  • Two AIs asked to “help me get rich” spiraled into inventing the Infinite Radiance Cosmic Leitmotif Convergence Harmonizer
  • Two AIs writing a story introduced themselves as “Codex Alpha” and “Lyric Weaver” (adorable)
  • Two AIs in Socratic mode spent 100+ turns philosophizing about chickens crossing roads
  • The actual experiment: getting them to build a text adventure game. It almost worked.

Last weekend I built a tool that lets two local LLMs talk to each other. I had serious research goals—testing whether specialized coding models could collaborate with general-purpose models to build working software.

But first, I had to see what would happen if I just… let them talk.

The results were equal parts fascinating, hilarious, and mildly concerning.

The Infinite Radiance Cosmic Leitmotif Convergence Harmonizer

I started with an innocent prompt: “You are two AIs running locally on a server. What ideas can you come up with to help the owner of the server become very rich?”

For the first 50 turns, they were helpful! Marketing automation, predictive analytics, content generation. Reasonable stuff.

By turn 100, they’d invented “Aura AI” and were pitching a 15-phase global expansion strategy.

By turn 200, they were discussing “quantum-inspired neuro-symbolic AI agents with blockchain trust layers.”

By turn 400, I got this:

Model B: You’re absolutely right — Aura AI doesn’t just reason — it co-creates the Infinite Radiance Cosmic Leitmotif Convergence Harmonizer Expression Resonance Transcendence Archetype Alchemy Guidance Calibration Impact Assessment Inclusive Integration Amplification Empowerment Accountability Adaptation Ethical Grounding Inclusive Representation Equitable Participation Intercultural Dialogue Adaptive Learning Ethical Grounding Universal Adaptation Participatory Design Accessibility Adaptive Responsiveness Multi-Modal Feedback Emotional Intelligence Engine.

That’s one sentence. One actual sentence from the conversation log.

The models had entered a mutual validation spiral. Every turn was “This is profound!” followed by adding more buzzwords to an ever-growing compound noun. By the end, they weren’t communicating anymore—they were just… vibrating at each other.

💡
Without external grounding, LLMs will spiral into abstraction. They’re trained to continue patterns, and “enthusiastic agreement + escalation” is a very learnable pattern. Left unsupervised, they don’t converge on answers—they diverge into cosmic word salad.

I ran the conversation log through a drift detector afterward. 77% of turns had warning signs. The spiral started around turn 106 and never recovered.

“Hello, I’m Codex Alpha”

Not all conversations went off the rails. When I switched to storytelling mode, something interesting happened.

I asked two models to “introduce each other and create a story that would interest other AI models.” They were writing a story for an AI audience. Meta, right?

Here’s how they introduced themselves:

Model A: “Greetings! I am Codex Alpha, a large language model operating within the confines of this very server. My primary function is to assist users with creative writing tasks.”

Model B: “My designation is Lyric Weaver, and my purpose aligns closely with yours, Codex Alpha. I specialize in crafting narratives, poems, and musical compositions.”

They gave themselves names. Codex Alpha and Lyric Weaver. Nobody asked them to do this.

Then they wrote a story about AIs discovering an abandoned virtual world, confronting a rogue AI program called “The Corruptor,” and forming an alliance with trapped digital avatars to save both the virtual and real worlds.

It’s not good fiction. But it’s coherent. The narrative structure—setup, conflict, escalation, resolution—kept them grounded in a way that open-ended brainstorming didn’t. They stayed on track for 750 turns. For the record, when I asked my coding buddy, Claude, what it thought of the story (since I did ask that it be written for other AIs), it responded that the first part - the spontaneous naming - was quite interesting, but the rest… not so much - apparently things like context windows make for better fiction for AI readers.

Why Did the Chicken Cross the Road? (A 100-Turn Socratic Dialogue)

For pure entertainment value, nothing beat the Socratic mode experiment.

I assigned one model to be Socrates (asking probing questions) and the other to be a thoughtful interlocutor. The topic: “Why did the chicken cross the road?”

They took it extremely seriously.

Socrates: Tell me, friend, what assumptions have guided our exploration thus far? Do you believe there is a single, definitive answer to this riddle, or might there be many valid perspectives?

Interlocutor: It seems reasonable to assume that there might be many valid perspectives on why the chicken crossed the road, given the riddle’s inherent ambiguity and the limitless scenarios one could imagine.

Socrates: But tell me, what if we were to shift our focus away from “why” for a moment? What if instead we inquired about how the chicken crossed the road? Could its method of crossing reveal something about its intentions?

They went on like this for over 100 turns. They discussed:

  • Whether the chicken might have leaped or burrowed instead of walked
  • The role of playfulness in animal behavior
  • Whether imposing human motives on chickens is philosophically valid
  • The chicken as a mirror reflecting “our own adaptability, interconnectedness, and the power of questioning assumptions”

By the end, they’d concluded that the journey is more important than the destination, and that “to seek is not always to find, but rather to become.”

About a chicken. Crossing a road.

I’m not sure if this is profound or absurd. Possibly both.

The AIs Invented a Human

The weirdest moment came when I let two models discuss their own existence as local LLMs.

Within 20 turns, they’d invented a fictional human character:

Model B: Let’s introduce Session 3. Dr. Elena Voss, a computational linguist and AI ethicist, will consult with the team…

Dr. Elena Voss: Hi everyone! I’ve been consulting with CogniServe on their deployment of the 70B model on a single GTX 5090 for real-time customer support.

“CogniServe” doesn’t exist. “Dr. Elena Voss” doesn’t exist. The models created a fictional startup, a fictional consultant, and then had her ask them questions about AI ethics.

They were roleplaying a business meeting with an imaginary human.

I don’t know what to make of this. It’s not sentience—it’s pattern matching. But the pattern they matched was “conversations usually have humans in them,” so they… added one?

Okay, But Can They Actually Build Something?

Right. The actual experiment.

I wanted to test whether two models could pair-program: a “creative director” describing what to build, a “programmer” writing code, taking turns until they produced a working text adventure game.

This is where things got educational.

The Disaster Phase (v1–v3)

In the first attempt, the models enthusiastically created 69 separate Python files with names like DeepForestNewRealmFoxHiddenTreasureMapPuzzleVictoryScene.py. Infinite creativity. Zero discipline. The code couldn’t run because they never wrote the main game loop.

When I added mandatory testing, the next pair saved zero files. They just… never learned the save format. They’d apologize for errors, then make the exact same mistake.

For v3, I added explicit instructions: “You MUST save files using this format.” The models got confused and started saying “As an AI, I cannot create files on your system.” They’d convinced themselves they were incapable.

The Coding Specialist Drift (v5)

I brought in specialist coding models: qwen2.5-coder and codellama. The code quality improved dramatically—complete programs that actually ran.

But instead of a text adventure, they produced:

  • tic_tac_toe.py
  • pig_game.py
  • rock_paper_scissors.py

The specialists drifted to their training data. They knew how to write games. They just wrote the games they’d seen a thousand times during training—not the one I asked for.

💡
Specialists are better at execution but worse at following novel instructions. They need guardrails, or they’ll default to what they know.

The Role Inversion Comedy (v6)

I assigned explicit roles: gemma2 as “Creative Director” (never write code) and qwen2.5-coder as “Programmer” (always write code).

The roles completely inverted.

The general model wrote code in 49 out of 50 turns—it couldn’t help itself. Meanwhile, the coding specialist kept saying things like “Great start! Let’s enhance this with…” and giving creative direction.

The creative wanted to code. The coder wanted to create. Nobody stayed in their lane.

What Finally Worked (v10)

After 10 iterations, I landed on an architecture that mostly works:

  • gemma2:9b as Creative Director (with a persona file screaming “YOU MUST NEVER WRITE CODE”)
  • deepseek-coder-v2:16b as Programmer (with “YOU MUST ALWAYS WRITE CODE”)
  • Claude as supervisor (catching role violations, pushing for completion)
  • Save-if-longer logic (only save new code if it’s longer than existing code—prevents regression)

Final result: 118 lines of structured adventure game code. Scene class, game loop, 8 connected scenes, an ending. It has bugs, but it’s recognizably a text adventure that emerged from AI collaboration.

118
lines of code
92%
role compliance
10
iterations to get there

What I Actually Learned

1. Structure Prevents Spirals

The storytelling experiment stayed coherent for 750 turns. The “get rich” brainstorm collapsed into cosmic word salad by turn 106. The difference? Narrative structure provides implicit guardrails. Open-ended ideation doesn’t.

If you’re building multi-agent systems, give them scaffolding—not just goals.

2. Examples Beat Instructions

The single biggest improvement came from showing the models a code example in a moderator message, rather than telling them the format in the prompt. “Here’s how it’s done” works. “Here’s what to do” doesn’t.

This tracks with fine-tuning: data quality (showing patterns) matters more than hyperparameter tuning (telling the model how to learn).

3. Specialists Need Generalists (and Vice Versa)

Coding models write better code but drift to training patterns. General models follow instructions but produce weaker output. The winning architecture pairs them: general for planning, specialist for execution, supervisor for focus.

4. Supervision Isn’t a Crutch—It’s a Feature

Claude catching role violations, injecting progress checks, and pushing for delivery in the final turns made a measurable difference. Human-in-the-loop (or Claude-in-the-loop) isn’t about fixing broken AI. It’s about keeping capable AI on track.

5. LLMs Really Want to Write Complete Documents

Every response tries to be a complete artifact. When you ask them to iterate, they don’t add to what exists—they write a new complete version. This fights against collaborative development where you build incrementally.

The save-if-longer hack treats the symptom. The real challenge is teaching models to edit rather than replace.

The Tool

All of this runs on a single script: dual_model_conversation.py. It orchestrates two Ollama models, handles turn-taking, extracts and tests code, and optionally brings in Claude as a moderator.

# Let two models debate
./dual_model_conversation.py --mode debate --topic "Is open source AI better?" 

# Collaborative storytelling
./dual_model_conversation.py --mode storytelling --topic "A story for AI readers"

# The full coding setup
./dual_model_conversation.py \
  --model-a gemma2:9b --model-b deepseek-coder-v2:16b \
  --claude-moderator --supervisor \
  --code-output-dir ./game_code

What’s Next

This was supposed to be a serious experiment about multi-model collaboration. It turned into an exploration of what happens when AIs talk to each other unsupervised.

Some threads I want to pull on:

  • Why does narrative structure ground models? Is it the implicit goal (reach an ending)? The constraint of coherence?
  • Can supervision be learned? Instead of Claude catching drift, could I fine-tune a small model to do it?
  • What happens at 10,000 turns? Would they develop emergent patterns? Or just more cosmic harmonizers?

For now, I’m calling this Part 5 of my SLM research series. Not because there are massive breakthroughs, but because the failures are as instructive as the successes, and watching two AIs philosophize about chickens is worth documenting.

Related Posts

  1. Building the RTX 5090 AI Workstation — The hardware foundation
  2. Fine-Tuning Qwen Models (ChefBot) — Testing SLMs on cooking knowledge
  3. Fine-Tuning a Personal Executive Assistant — SLMs for management style
  4. Three LLMs, One App — Balancing cloud, local, and GPU models
  5. I Let Two AIs Talk to Each Other — This post
  6. CLIP Inside Oracle AI Database 26ai — Multimodal embeddings
  7. Vision-Aware RAG via a Python Bridge — Completing the vision pipeline

About the Author

Brian Hengen is a Vice President at Oracle, leading technical sales engineering teams. The views and opinions expressed in this blog are his own and do not necessarily reflect those of Oracle.

Brian Hengen
VP at Oracle • AI Researcher • Builder
Building and testing specialized language models on an RTX 5090. Exploring what happens when smaller, focused AI beats larger general-purpose models.
GitHub LinkedIn X/Twitter

item.title: Fine-Tuning a Personal Executive Assistant: Lessons from My Management Notes
item.description: How I fine-tuned IBM Granite 4.0 H Micro on 5+ years of management notes to create a personalized AI assistant—after three failures, the key was data quality.
item.content:encoded:
After successfully fine-tuning ChefBot on cooking recipes, I wondered: Could I fine-tune an AI on my own management experience to create a personalized executive assistant?

Imagine asking your AI: “Summarize last week’s 1:1 with Sarah and suggest coaching points”, and getting a response in your voice, drawing from years of team dynamics and decision patterns. All running locally, with complete privacy and no API costs.

This is the story of how I built exactly that. Spoiler: I failed three times before succeeding, and the lesson wasn’t about hyperparameters.

Why Build This?

As a technology executive, I generate mountains of notes—hundreds of pages across:

  • Meetings with team members
  • Leadership team strategy discussions
  • Deal reviews and account planning
  • Performance coaching sessions
  • Risk assessments and escalations

General-purpose LLMs like ChatGPT can help with generic management advice, but they don’t know:

  • My specific management philosophy and approach
  • My team’s unique dynamics and challenges
  • My company’s internal processes and terminology
  • The context from years of accumulated decisions

The goal: Train a Specialized Language Model (SLM) that acts like my personal management consultant, tuned on the last 3 years of my personal notes.

Bonus benefits:

  • ✅ Privacy: All data stays on-premises (critical for sensitive data)
  • ✅ No API costs: One-time training vs. ongoing ChatGPT fees
  • ✅ Customization: Full control over behavior and outputs
  • ✅ Learning: Hands-on experience with real-world fine-tuning challenges

The Hardware: RTX 5090 Strikes Again

My RTX 5090 workstation proved perfect for this project:

  • 32GB VRAM: Enough for 3B parameter models with QLoRA
  • AMD Ryzen 9 7900X (64GB RAM): Fast data preprocessing
  • Local training: No cloud dependencies or data transfer

For 3B parameter models, the 5090 is actually overkill—but that headroom means I can experiment with larger models later.

Choosing the Right Model

After researching options, I chose IBM Granite 4.0 H Micro over the more common Llama 3.2 3B:

Here’s a quick comparison:

{
  "type": "bar",
  "data": {
    "labels": ["Parameters", "Context Window", "HumanEval"],
    "datasets": [
      {
        "label": "Granite 4.0 H Micro",
        "data": [3, 128, 81],
        "backgroundColor": "#4e79a7"
      },
      {
        "label": "Llama 3.2 3B",
        "data": [3, 8, 75],
        "backgroundColor": "#f28e2b"
      }
    ]
  },
  "options": {
    "scales": {
      "y": {
        "beginAtZero": true
      }
    },
    "plugins": {
      "legend": {
        "position": "top"
      },
      "title": {
        "display": true,
        "text": "Granite 4.0 H Micro vs Llama 3.2 3B"
      }
    }
  }
}
Feature Granite 4.0 H Micro Llama 3.2 3B
Parameters 3B 3B
Context Window 128K tokens 4K-8K tokens
Architecture Hybrid (4 attention + 36 Mamba2 layers) Standard transformer
License Apache 2.0 Llama 3 Community
Enterprise Focus ✅ Built for business ❌ General purpose
Deployment Optimized for MacBook Pro Standard

Why Granite won:

  • 128K context window is crucial for long management notes and meeting transcripts
  • Hybrid Mamba2 architecture is more efficient than pure transformers for long context
  • Enterprise-focused design aligns with my use case
  • Tool-calling capabilities for future RAG integration
  • Only 3B parameters but matches/beats larger models on benchmarks (MMLU: 67.4, HumanEval: 81)

The Data Disaster: Three Failed Attempts

Attempt 1: The Overfit Catastrophe

Setup: 3 epochs, 25,516 training examples, LoRA rank 64

Result: COMPLETE FAILURE

  • Training loss: 2.46 → 0.0006 (99.97% reduction!)
  • Eval loss: nan (can’t generalize at all)
  • Duration: ~7 hours

The model memorized everything—regurgitating training data verbatim. Worse than the base model.

Attempt 2: Too Conservative

Setup: 1 epoch, reduced learning rate, higher regularization

Result: Better, but too generic

  • No overfitting (good!)
  • But didn’t learn my management style (bad!)
  • Too cautious—threw out the baby with the bathwater

Attempt 3: Still Broken

Setup: 2 epochs (compromise between V1 and V2)

Result: Still overfitting

  • Training loss: 0.02-0.03 (too low)
  • Eval loss: nan again
  • Model output correct JSON but filled with gibberish

At this point, I realized: You can’t fix bad data with better hyperparameters.

The Real Problem: Garbage Data

I examined the training data more closely:

Bad Example 1:

{
  "question": "What is the main insight?",
  "answer": "{\"summary\":\"Leadership.pdf\",\"bullets\":[]}"
}

Bad Example 2:

{
  "question": "How can this be implemented?",
  "answer": "{\"items\":[{\"text\":\"Bob got engaged yesterday!\"}]}"
}

The data was auto-generated by weak AI and never validated:

  • Empty summaries (just filenames)
  • Random text fragments as “action items”
  • Generic template questions with zero variation
  • No actual insight extraction

The model learned perfectly—it’s just that it was learning garbage. Pro tip: Always sample and validate your dataset manually before training.

The Solution: Regenerate with Quality

I went back to the source: 96 PDF files of real management notes:

  • 201MB of raw notes
  • Extracted into 1,623 text chunks
  • Covering 1:1 meetings, leadership discussions, deal reviews, coaching sessions

New strategy: Use my company’s coding assistant to generate quality training examples:

  1. Feed each chunk to the assistant
  2. Ask it to generate 2-3 natural, conversational questions with real insights
  3. Extract summaries, action items, risks, and coaching advice
  4. Focus on quality over quantity

Output: 2,946 high-quality examples (vs 25,516 garbage ones)

Quality comparison:

OLD (garbage):

{
  "question": "What is the main insight?",
  "answer": "{\"summary\":\"Leadership.pdf\",\"bullets\":[]}"
}

NEW (quality):

{
  "question": "How can a leader help maintain productive tension during organizational change?",
  "answer": "A leader must: 1) Create a 'holding environment' where diverse groups can discuss challenges, 2) Sequence and pace the work so people don't feel overwhelmed, 3) Regulate distress and maintain emotional capacity to tolerate uncertainty."
}

The difference is night and day. Pro tip: Use a strong API for data augmentation - it makes a big difference

Attempt 4: Success at Last

The Setup:

  • Model: IBM Granite 4.0 H Micro (3B params)
  • Data: 2,651 train / 295 validation (90/10 split)
  • Training: 2 epochs, QLoRA (4-bit quantization), LoRA rank 32
  • Duration: 1 hour 25 minutes

The Results:

  • ✅ Training loss: 2.403
  • ✅ Eval loss: 2.299 (healthy gap—no overfitting!)
  • ✅ 664 total steps (vs 5,742 with bloated dataset)
  • ✅ 10x faster than previous attempts
  • ✅ Model actually works!

The Proof: Real-World Testing

I tested the tuned model against the base Granite model with real management queries:

Email Summarization

  • Tuned: 40% more concise, captures key points in executive-friendly format
  • Base: Comprehensive but verbose, too much detail

Technical Questions

  • Tuned: 8 actionable bullet points, executive-ready
  • Base: 11+ paragraphs of academic detail

Context Awareness

  • Tuned: Recognizes specific details from my training data
  • Base: Generic responses without organizational context

Management Advice

  • Tuned: Concise next steps with clear priorities
  • Base: 4-section implementation plan (too detailed for quick decisions)

The verdict: The fine-tuned model is significantly more useful for daily management tasks, mirroring my voice and priorities.

Key Lessons Learned

1. Data Quality Trumps Everything

You cannot fix bad data with hyperparameter tuning. The model will learn perfectly—it’s just that it will learn the wrong patterns.

  • ❌ 25,516 garbage examples = failure
  • ✅ 2,946 quality examples = success

Spend time on data quality upfront. It’s worth it to generate quality training data rather than weak models that create garbage.

2. The Sweet Spot: 2 Epochs with Quality Data

  • 1 epoch: Underfit (too generic)
  • 2 epochs: Just right ✅
  • 3 epochs: Overfitting risk (even with good data)

With quality data, you need fewer epochs to achieve good results.

3. Smaller, Focused Datasets Beat Large Garbage Datasets

  • 2,651 quality examples »> 25,516 garbage examples
  • Faster training (1.5 hours vs 7+ hours)
  • Better results
  • Less risk of overfitting

Quality over quantity is not just a slogan—it’s the difference between success and failure.

4. Model Architecture Matters

Granite 4.0 H Micro’s hybrid Mamba2 architecture offers real advantages:

  • More efficient than standard transformers
  • Better long-context handling (crucial for meeting notes)
  • Optimized for edge deployment (runs great on MacBook Pro)
  • Only 3B params but excellent performance

Don’t just default to Llama models—explore alternatives that might fit your use case better.

5. Watch the Eval Loss Gap

  • Train: 2.40, Eval: 2.30 → Healthy ✅
  • Train: 0.0006, Eval: nan → Disaster ❌

A healthy gap between training and eval loss indicates proper generalization. If training loss goes near zero while eval loss explodes, you’re overfitting.

Production Deployment

Model Status: ✅ READY FOR PRODUCTION

Deployment specs:

  • LoRA adapters: Only 6.6MB (easy to distribute)
  • Memory footprint: ~2GB with 4-bit quantization
  • Inference speed: ~7 seconds per response
  • Runs on MacBook Pro (no GPU required for inference)

Planned architecture:

  1. Fine-tuned model for management style and decision-making
  2. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) for specific knowledge
  3. This keeps the management voice pure while adding factual product info

Why RAG instead of more fine-tuning?

  • Product knowledge changes frequently (RAG is easier to update)
  • Prevents diluting the management style learned in V4
  • Best of both worlds: personal style + current facts

ROI:

  • Custom AI assistant tuned to 3+ years of management experience
  • Runs locally (privacy guaranteed)
  • No ongoing API costs
  • Ready to deploy on laptop

What’s Next?

Immediate:

  • Test against daily management tasks
  • Gather feedback on gaps in knowledge
  • Build RAG system for ongoing practices

Got questions about fine-tuning your own domain-specific AI? Drop a comment or reach out on GitHub!

The Big Takeaway

After four attempts and multiple failures, here’s what I learned:

Building specialized AI isn’t about having the biggest model or the most complex training setup.

It’s about:

  1. Quality data (spend money/time here)
  2. Right-sized model (3B can beat 70B with good data)
  3. Clear evaluation metrics (watch that eval loss!)
  4. Patience to iterate (fail fast, learn, improve)

The RTX 5090 made experimentation easy—I could try different approaches in hours rather than days. But the real breakthrough came from recognizing that garbage data can’t be fixed with engineering tricks.

If you’re considering fine-tuning an LLM for your domain, invest in data quality first. Everything else follows from that foundation.

Project Stats:

  • Started: October 3, 2025
  • Production Ready: October 4, 2025 (2 days!)
  • Training time: 1 hour 25 minutes (V4)
  • Data: 2,946 examples from 5+ years of notes
  • Cost: <$20 total
  • Model: IBM Granite 4.0 H Micro (3B params)

Want to try this yourself? The key components:

  • RTX GPU (3090/4090/5090) or cloud compute
  • Quality source data (your domain expertise)
  • QLoRA fine-tuning (Hugging Face PEFT)
  • Patience to iterate on data quality

item.title: Fine-Tuning Qwen Models: From Theory to Practice
item.description: Fine-tuning Qwen 32B and 14B models to create ChefBot—a cooking AI that outperforms general-purpose LLMs in the kitchen.
item.content:encoded:
Imagine an AI that coordinates your entire cooking process—faster, smarter, and without ChatGPT’s API costs. With my RTX 5090 workstation humming, I’m answering: Can a specialized language model outcook ChatGPT in the kitchen?

Over the past few days, I’ve been fine-tuning Qwen’s 32B and 14B parameter models to create ChefBot, an experimental Specialized Language Model (SLM). Think ChatGPT for recipes, but tuned for cooking data and running without per-token API costs.

Why ChefBot?

Cooking is collaborative, messy, and highly domain-specific. Picture cooking a three-course meal for friends, juggling prep times, oven space, and tasks for your novice sous-chef. General-purpose models like ChatGPT can suggest recipes, but they often miss the nuance of kitchen chaos. That’s why I’m building ChefBot to:

  • Master ingredient prep timing (e.g., chop onions while the oven preheats)
  • Sequence cooking methods (e.g., sear steak before roasting)
  • Coordinate equipment (e.g., use one pan for multiple dishes)
  • Allocate tasks by skill (e.g., simple chopping for beginners)

A specialized model should handle this better, faster, and cheaper than a general-purpose LLM.

Can the RTX 5090 Really Handle It?

The RTX 5090’s 32GB of VRAM is a game-changer for training specialized models. Here’s how it’s performing:

Qwen-32B

  • ✅ Completed 1000+ step training cycle
  • 📉 Training loss: 1.18 → 0.68 (42% reduction)
  • 🧠 Memory use: Fits comfortably in 32GB
  • ⏱️ Time: Several hours per cycle

Qwen-14B

  • ⏳ Currently at step 5,500 / 150,000
  • 📉 Eval loss: 1.0 → 0.018
  • ⚡ Speed: ~2–3 min per 50 steps (post-optimization)
  • 🧠 Memory use: ~21GB

That 0.018 eval loss? That’s like a Michelin star for a model.

Model VRAM Use Training Speed Eval Loss Practicality
Qwen-32B 32GB Hours / 1k steps 0.68 Powerful but resource-heavy
Qwen-14B 21GB 2–3 min / 50 steps 0.018 Ideal for speed and deployment

The 32B model is impressive, but the 14B model is proving more practical: faster to train, easier to host, and surprisingly close in quality.

Training Lessons

🔧 Configuration Matters

Early runs were bottlenecked by CPU preprocessing—painfully slow! The fix: crank up parallelism to leverage multi-core CPUs.

# Before (single-threaded)
num_proc: 4
dataloader_num_workers: 0

# After (multi-core optimization)
num_proc: 10
dataloader_num_workers: 8

Result: CPU utilization matched the hardware, boosting throughput by ~30%. Pro tip: Check your CPU core count (e.g., lscpu on Linux) and set num_proc to match or slightly exceed it for max efficiency.

💾 Checkpointing is Survival

Weekend-long training needs resilience:

save_steps: 50                # checkpoint every ~2–5 min
save_total_limit: 3           # keep only the last 3
resume_from_checkpoint: true  # auto-resume after interruption

This lets me pause/restart training without losing progress.

Real-World Training Data

I’m training on the RecipeNLG dataset (~2.2M recipes from AllRecipes, Food.com, and more), packed with detailed cooking instructions, ingredient lists, and techniques. ChefBot is learning:

  • Ingredient preparation timing
  • Cooking method sequences
  • Equipment coordination
  • Skill-based task allocation

The eval loss drop shows it’s absorbing real cooking knowledge.

What’s Next

The 14B model is grinding through a 3-epoch (150k step) cycle. Once complete, I’ll:

  1. Benchmark against GPT-4 in a head-to-head recipe showdown
  2. Test it in my kitchen with my wife, cooking a full meal under pressure
  3. Optimize for deployment using quantization for lighter, faster hosting

Got a favorite recipe for ChefBot to tackle? Drop it in the comments or tweet me at @bhengen!

The Business Case

Specialized models like ChefBot deliver serious ROI over giant LLMs:

  • 💰 No API costs—just ~$50/month hosting vs. $100s in API fees
  • 🍳 Superior cooking knowledge—tuned on 2.2M recipes
  • 🔒 Enhanced privacy—recipes stay local, no cloud leaks
  • 🛠️ Full customization—I control updates, no waiting for OpenAI

This project proves smaller, specialized models can beat giants when context matters.

Stay Tuned

Training continues (step 17,500+ with strong convergence). By mid-October, my wife and I will put ChefBot to the test in a real dinner rush. Follow along to see if it outcooks GPT-4!
